summary:
The Battle of Adwa on 1 March 1896 was one of the most significant military engagements in African and world history: the decisive defeat of an Italian colonial army by the forces of Ethiopian Emperor Menelik II, making Ethiopia the only African nation to successfully repel a European colonial invasion and maintain its independence through the entire colonial period. The battle sent shockwaves through European capitals and stands as a defining moment of African resistance to imperialism.

Italy had been seeking to establish a colonial empire in the Horn of Africa, gaining control of Eritrea on the Red Sea coast. The Treaty of Wuchale (1889), signed with Ethiopia, contained an article whose Amharic and Italian versions differed crucially: the Italian version claimed that Ethiopia had accepted Italian protectorate status, while the Amharic version merely provided an option to use Italian diplomatic services. When Menelik II repudiated Italy's protectorate claim, war became inevitable.

The Italian force under General Oreste Baratieri that invaded northern Ethiopia numbered approximately 17,700 men, including both Italian and Eritrean troops. Menelik assembled a large Ethiopian army of perhaps 70,000–100,000 warriors, well-armed with modern rifles (many supplied by Russia and France, who were happy to see Italy humiliated) and led by experienced commanders including Empress Taytu and several prominent regional governors (Rases).

At Adwa, Baratieri divided his force into four columns that lost coordination and were defeated piecemeal by the Ethiopian forces. Approximately 6,000–7,000 Italians were killed and 1,500 captured — nearly half the invading force — in the worst defeat ever inflicted by an African army on a European colonial force. The Treaty of Addis Ababa that followed recognized Ethiopian independence and Italy's renunciation of its protectorate claims. Adwa became an inspiration for African independence movements throughout the twentieth century and remains a national holiday in Ethiopia.
